text: Hi, I'm Vahag Nicolian from OHSU, and this is a video of a robotic tap inguinal hernia repair in a patient who presented with a giant inguinal scrotal hernia. This patient was actually referred to me by my urology colleagues. He had presented to their clinic with a right renal mass that needed a partial nephrectomy. And the CT scan for workup was noted to have a large hernia He was a seventy-nine-year-old gentleman with a history of significant dementia, surgical history for open appendectomy. After discussing with my neurology colleagues, we elected to actually have him undergo his robotic right partial nephrectomy, which identified a benign growth, and then we did a staged hernia repair for essentially a reducible hernia.

He had a CT scan. You'll note a very large hernia on the left side. and then a hernia on the right side that was essentially made up of preperitoneal fat through the direct and indirect spaces. In this particular case, I had a very thorough discussion with the patients and his family. His medical decision maker, who was his wife, wanted to avoid a bilateral repair as his contralateral side had no symptoms. Obviously, a large amount of viscera within this hernia. In the clinic, we were able to reduce the vast majority of that left inguinal hernia. You see a large defect there. No bladder was involved in this hernia. And so we proceeded with a minimally invasive approach with a low threshold to convert to hybrid. Induction of anesthesia, we were able to reduce the majority of his hernia. When we went in laparoscopically, we identified that there was still a portion of incarcerated colon, which we were able to manually reduce. We see a large defect through the deep ring.

The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon begins the operation by developing the peritoneal flap in a standard manner. Preperitoneal dissection is initiated, followed by medial dissection towards the retropubic space. The surgeon encounters adhesive bands between the colon and the hernia sac, opting to minimize dissection in this area. The medial dissection continues, identifying the pubis and transitioning into the preperitoneal space. The peritoneum appears thin during the dissection process.

The surgeon inverts a large hernia sac with gentle counter tension, facilitating the dissection and improving orientation. Small fenestrations in the redundant sac are visible, and adhesions are lysed, revealing a pseudosac. The operation progresses towards establishing the critical view of the myopectenial orifice.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks and creates a pocket for an extra-large heavyweight mesh during the retropubic dissection. Parietalization of structures is performed, and portions of the hernia sac are addressed. The mesh is positioned over the defect and fixated at several points.

The surgeon closes the superior peritoneal flap, addressing the redundancy in the hernia sac by plicating it to prevent inversion and potential obstruction. The sac is integrated into the peritoneum, minimizing dead space with stitches placed in various directions.

Again, this patient was kept overnight for observation, did wonderful, required no narcotics, we placed no drain, we just did compression undergarments, just given his medical history, and he did remarkably well. I think for any patient with an inguinal scleral hernia, you can approach these robotically or MIS. I think there has to be some branch points regarding an open or hybrid approach, especially for larger defects that may need anterior application. Drains can be used selectively. We didn't use it in this case, but definitely low threshold to use one. And in scenarios where there's significant adhesions between the sac And the canal, it's likely worthwhile to consider abandonment of the sac to avoid complications like hematomas. Thank you very much.
